parts.text:
The open-plan office fell into a vacuum of silence as Arthur stood over Miller’s desk. Arthur didn’t shout; he spoke with the calm, melodic clarity of a man describing a sunset, which made it infinitely worse.

“Miller,” Arthur said, tapping a single finger on Miller’s monitor. “I’ve spent the last ten minutes looking at this spreadsheet, and I’ve come to a fascinating realization. You aren’t actually a human being, are you? You’re just a collection of bad habits held together by a cheap polyester blend.”

Miller opened his mouth to speak, but Arthur held up a hand. 

“Don’t. Every time you breathe, you’re essentially stealing oxygen from people who might actually contribute something to the species today. I’ve seen mold grow with more ambition than you. I’ve seen inanimate objects—staplers, paperweights, decorative ferns—demonstrate a higher level of professional competence than whatever this pathetic display is.”

Arthur gestured vaguely at the screen, then turned to the rest of the room, who were all staring at their keyboards with desperate intensity.

“Look at him, everyone,” Arthur announced. “Take a good look at the face of a man who reached his intellectual peak in the third grade and has been in a steady, pathetic decline ever since. Miller is our office’s very own participation trophy. He’s the physical embodiment of a shrug.”

“I just thought—” Miller began, his voice cracking.

“That’s your first mistake. You don’t have the equipment for ‘thought,’ Miller. You have a damp sponge between your ears that occasionally sparks when you remember to blink. You are an embarrassment to the very concept of labor. If I replaced you with a bucket of lukewarm water, the department’s productivity would go up, if only because the bucket wouldn’t require a health insurance plan or have such a punchable, vacant expression.”

Arthur leaned in closer, his voice dropping to a jagged whisper.

“You’re a placeholder, Miller. A mistake we haven’t gotten around to erasing yet. You’re the human equivalent of a typo. Now, sit there, try not to drool on the equipment, and remain as invisible as your talent. If I hear so much as a sigh out of you, I’ll have your desk moved into the parking garage so you can be closer to the other trash.”

Arthur patted Miller’s shoulder twice—a gesture so condescending it felt like a physical assault—and strolled back to his glass office without looking back.
